video_title: Wellum’s Warning: The Tech Boom Is Starting to Crack
transcript: The internet has changed our world, but people overpaid. Are you paying for a dream or are you paying for a real business? So, be prepared to be disappointed. That's really the big issue, the sky-high expectations. Will you take a capital loss and you have a set setback for maybe 3 to 4 years. Speculative fever, FOMO, I can't miss out. We're seeing the same thing again and that means caution. >> Hello and welcome to Wealthion. I'm Maggie Lake. Joining me today to discuss how to find value in this market is Jonathan Wellum, CEO and CIO of RockLink. Good morning, Jonathan. >> Good morning, Maggie. I think you're picking on me here. You're picking some of the tough toughest times to get on here and start talking about what we're up to, but >> That's why we need people like you. That's exactly what we need is somebody on the other end of the phone. But but we really appreciate you taking the time. And if you are listening to this, I'm sure you're feeling these tough times. It's volatile. There's a lot going on. There's a lot of sort of conflict in the narrative. So, if you want to sort of stress test your portfolio, figure out if you're on the right track, you can get a free review from an advisor in our network like Jonathan. You can just go to wealthion.com/free. We all need a little help. So, you're going to you're going to sort of help at least put my mind at ease, Jonathan. And you know, we have we have a wild investing environment we're in. Even even sort of market veterans and long-time folks who've been doing this like yourself have been saying, "Wow, this is a lot." We have big price swings in sectors like semiconductor and AI-related names, giant IPOs coming to market. And I think a lot of investors are struggling with the question, is this a bubble or is this the future? Should I have exposure or am I chasing a top? You're a value-based investor. That's sort of the framework you use. How are you tackling these questions? >> Yeah, good question. We you know, we obviously get from people. When you start to see a sub indices like the semiconductor index go up, you know, 70 plus percent year-to-date, things like this, and you start to see these parabolic moves, and you just you have this endless amount of capital going into AI, AI, data centers, data centers, you have to take a deep breath and step back. I mean, this is classic over investing, um you know, chasing certain markets up. And the only way we can go and uh approach it is to go back to valuations and try to separate the hype from the durable value of these businesses because in many of these businesses, there is a durable value being created, clearly. I mean, SpaceX over time could be uh certainly one of the largest market cap companies in the world. Um you know, the uh Anthropic and uh and AI-related businesses, these are serious companies. But you got to step try to separate the hype from the true value. So, you got to look at the price. What are you paying for these things? Um you have to be uh careful about looking at uh uh you know, how much they can grow, the forecast, what what's it dependent upon. Um you have to be careful again that you're not just chasing the chasing the top, the hype. Um if you look at a lot of these IPOs, they're coming out because valuations are good. Don't fool yourself. Yes, they can go higher, but they could also go lower for a while before they go higher. And that's typically what happens with these major IPOs. Um the the the track record is not great in the short run. In fact, you know, if you run the numbers, some people said that, you know, if you take a lot of the major IPOs, they'll be down as much as 40 and you know, and 48% over the next number of months afterwards. I'm not saying that's going to happen here, but these guys who sell, you know, the seller knows more than the buyer. I heard that many, many years ago from a very astute investor, and he didn't like buying IPOs. He was a billionaire, very successful investor, and he said, you know, remember, when you're stepping into an IPO, there's not as much mar- information in the market, and it hasn't been looked at and and you know, scoured as much as some of the other ones and the sellers usually pick the time that is optimal for them. And so they are picking a high. It doesn't mean it's the high 5 years from now, but it could be the high at this point in time. So be very careful. You have to make sure that again you're looking at the underlying business, what's the potential? What are you paying for? Are you overpaying? Are you paying for a dream? Are you paying for a real business? And I think in some of the cases with some of these businesses it's very hard, very hard to justify the current prices. Um if you are, you're buying on momentum and you're buying on tremendously high expectations. So be prepared to be disappointed. If you're retail investing and you think I have to have something, that's not a good reason to buy it. It's a good reason to buy it when you can value it and it's appropriately valued and you understand the business and you understand where they're going to go in the future. A lot of these businesses as you know are changing very quickly. There's a rapid change in what's going on. Elon Musk is talking about a tremendous amount of things in the future, but those are future and they are dependent on technology, dependent on regulatory issues. Um and there will be uh bumps in the road. Um and that's not to say it's not going to go higher over time, but uh you could you in my view I would not be stepping in. I'd be waiting for a more better entry point and understanding the business a bit more before we stepped in. >> Yeah, I think you bring up such a good point and it's sort of the the existential question that we're grappling with and there might be a little generational issue in here as well because when we're looking at something brand new, right? Artificial intelligence, space exploration. I mean they're talking about data centers and you know, operating on on satellites. It's unknown, right? How do you assign value to something that's brand new? >> Yeah, yeah I it's very difficult and that's why I think people should be should be skeptical, a little bit skeptical when you're looking at these high high valuations because Uh, I mean you know, look, Elon Musk Musk has an amazing track record. He's an amazing individual. I I mean, yeah, yeah, I think it's fair to say he's probably a genius with what he's done. I mean, it's it's just hardly anybody like him. But, uh, he's been very successful with Tesla and, uh, you know, some of the robotics and automation and people are, you know, have about a 1.6 trillion plus valuation on Tesla. So, they're looking at that and going, "This is going to be also very successful." And it probably will be successful. But, that doesn't mean you pay a price that really maybe isn't isn't isn't the appropriate price for a couple more years or you might be disappointed. The market will take it down if it's if there's any kind of hiccup. And, uh, and so, that's where you just have to be get back to values, values, values and be very careful. Um, with some of these new technologies, I mean, the way you The answer your question specifically, when you look at companies where you aren't don't understand exactly what's going to happen and there's a lot of new technologies, you penalize them in your valuation. So, you'll put a higher discount rate, right? So, instead of using a discount rate where you, you know, you're discounting the future cash flows at maybe 7, 8% if it's well-known business like Coca-Cola or something like that, um, then you're going to look at these businesses and go, "No, it's more like a mining company here. There's a lot of risk. We're not sure how this is going to pan out." So, you're going to use a discount rate that might be 12, 13%. Well, if you're using a higher discount rate, as you know, you bring down the present value of those cash flows even if you're optimistic with those cash flows. That means the value of the business comes down. That's not what's happening here, right? What's happening here is people are paying high high price for these businesses and and they're not really discounting the risk. And, um, they're putting in, uh, you know, sort of blue sky. That might work, but there's a high probability you're going to get caught overpaying. And if you were just patient and waited for the hype to get out of the market, I think it's Some some people are saying it's three, four times oversubscribed. Let that clear out. Uh, there's going to be Google's raising money, Facebook's raising money, Entropic's raising money. There's a lot more money's going to be raised. There's going to be a lot of money moving around through the system and, uh, I think there's going to be many more opportunistic times to get in with a little bit more clarity in what's going on in the business. So, you've lowered your risk. I often say this to investors, if you're going to jump on an elevator and it's going to 100 floor, you don't necessarily have to jump on, you know, floor 1 2 3 4. You can get on at floor 20 if if it you're more comfortable that it's going to make it to 100. >> Mhm. >> So, um there's a long road of opportunity in a lot of these companies. So, be careful. The other thing I I'd point out is we're still trying to figure out how much value AI does add to businesses. We don't know. So, Google and some of the big hyperscalers are paying and saying they're going to pay, you know, a large annuity subscription fee for SpaceX to SpaceX every month to be able to get their services. And they're paying a lot now, but they can cancel those at any time and that that's dependent upon all of Google's clients actually wanting that product and Anthropic's clients wanting that product. If businesses on Main Street right across, you know, the economy figure that, hey, you know, AI, you know, it's added value, but maybe not as much. This is costing me a lot of money. Maybe I'm not going to pay as much for it. We we don't know what that's going to look like. So, right now everybody's I got to have AI. AI is going to drive productivity. It's going to drive cost down. Don't need as many people. And that's true to a certain extent, but we don't know what how full that extent is. And so, that also could mean that the your growth rates and the revenue that people are expecting might not be as fast as people expect. Might take a little more a little longer to assimilate it and for companies to justify those expenses because as you as you know, some of the companies already have spent a lot and they're saying, "Hey, wait a second. We spent our whole budget in the first four or five months of the year on AI and I we got to cut back." So, the point is there's a lot of uncertainty. We saw this with the internet Uh in 1999-2000. And the internet has changed our world, but people overpaid. >> Mhm. >> And when they overpaid, the Nasdaq, as you might probably remember, I lived through it. I was I was 10 years in the investment business at the time, and we were careful to stay clear of it. Um which we were very fortunate in the business I was managing at the time. And uh so the Nasdaq hit 5100, but then it dropped 78%. 78%. It didn't get back to 5100 until 2015. That's 15 years later. So again, the every situation is a little different, but there's some parallels there because, you know, you see explosive infrastructure capex frenzy at that time. You saw sky-high valuations. You saw market concentration in few companies. Speculative fever, FOMO, I can't miss out. >> Mhm. >> And then there was total uncertainty around the earnings. And so we're seeing the same thing again. And uh that means caution. Be careful. I'm not saying don't be buying, don't be looking, we're researching, we're looking at the space. We have some businesses that are tangential to the whole AI area, so we can try to, you know, take advantage of this rocket. But um be careful you're going to jump in with both feet and speculate or leverage into it. Uh be very careful. >> No, leverage especially. Um you're you're right. It's it's resisting that FOMO, right? Because there's just it's everywhere. We're seeing it. There's so much enthusiasm. And a lot of us would like to consider ourselves optimists, right? Where we are looking to the future. We want to be involved. But I think your point about patience is so important. That that dot-com bust, by the way, if you were caught in it, we lost a generation of people who wouldn't touch equities because their retirements got wiped out. It was really damaging to market psychology. And that's a long time to claw back from. >> And remember also there's survivor bias. So there's a lot of companies that were in that internet bubble that did not survive. They're gone. And so we're still looking at the companies that went through the internet bubble that did survive and have done quite well. You look at an Amazon, it was up and down and it's it's become a leader, right? But then there's a lot of other companies that were hyped at the time and they either were bought out at a very low price or disappeared. >> Yeah. >> And so we're going to see that again um in this whole space. Now, I'm not I'm not saying it's going to happen to the major big players, but uh if there's a lot of like smaller players and there'll be a lot of other players sort of trying to hold on to the and grab hold of this rocket ship. Uh there will be there will be uh you know, companies that aren't they don't survive. And that's fine. That's creative destruction. That's the that's the capital markets, but you just want to be careful you're not in the companies that uh you're overpaying for or maybe end up getting themselves into trouble. >> Yeah, and and sticking with lots of people say, well, I'm I'm I'm investing in the picks and axes. Like I'm in back investing in the Listen, you know, the people who laid all the fiber for the internet, a lot of them disappeared, too. So, I don't think you can just go on that and think that's where you're putting necessarily, you know, a value assignment on it. Uh when you you you know, the the issue for IPO is I think extremely important because it traditionally in the IPO market, um you know, some years back, maybe even as a decade ago, I think you could even say that. Um it was much more like you the risk was that this company would survive, right? It wasn't exactly a startup, but it had enough momentum and it was going to go to public market to continue to grow. That's where it was going to find its growth. Um and so your risk as an investor was I'm going to take a punt on this and it may be the next unicorn. Like it may be that kind of company. Now, and increasingly over the last decade, the the the companies that are coming to market are monsters, right? They've stayed private for a really long time and now they're coming to market. And I think for some people looking at this, they say, "Oh, well, this isn't hopium. They've got revenue. They've got a business plan. They're they they are the market leaders, so it's safer to step in and buy." Is that really the case or what do we need to understand about this sort of nature of these mega IPOs, these late-stage IPOs? >> Yeah, a couple things. I think you have to then ask yourself the question, why are they raising the capital? And are they doing it because it's opportunistic that the book value of the private investors is so low that they can take a little bit of money off the table. And we've seen that with CoreWeave, some of the other ones where, you know, they've gone public and then some of the major players have taken money off the table. Don't fool yourself. The folks that are bringing these companies to the market now, they know they're getting excellent valuations. They've made a lot of money. And so you then have to be concerned about, yes, I don't think the concern would be as much the business going out of business, but the sky-high expectations that are baked into these companies. That's that's the That's really the big issue, the sky-high expectations. Will they be able to fulfill or will you take a Will you take a capital loss and and be have a set setback for maybe 3 to 4 years? Are you You know, these things step back. Post IPO selling pressure should be interesting also. The lockup dynamics of some of these ones. There's a lot of There's a lot coming to the market that will put pressure on them. And then execution risk remains real in all of these business. Even even though they're larger, they're in a lot of new spaces. And they're they're they're This isn't, you know, proven areas, if you will. And so execution risk is still a big issue. And there'll be governance issues and and you know, government issues, regulatory issues that will also be coming down the pipe because these business is really large impact on the economy and on social fabric of our of our people. So those are There's There's still a lot of risks. Uh And then if you look at historical IPOs, yeah, the the math is not in your favor if you're jumping in at the right at the IPO. So yeah, those are all things you've got to keep in mind. And again, it's not pooh-poohing any of these companies. I think there's Some of them are amazing with they with they do. And I'm intrigued to see what Elon Musk can do with SpaceX. >> Mhm. And data centers in the in the sky, that's fantastic. If we can cut the cost and keep them in the sky and do it in an energy efficient way, fantastic. Have 10 times the speed and the things that he talks about, mining asteroids, that would be great if we could if we could do that. But again, these are all very much futuristic and are going to take some time. And there's lots of lots of things that can go wrong also along the way and that's that's the normal course of the business that's involved in new areas and and you know, run by somebody who's prepared to prepared to take risks. >> Yes, his time horizon's worth mentioning might be different than yours as an investor. He doesn't even like being in the public market because he has such a long time horizon, which I think again we respect him for in the genius around that. But you've got to line that up with with your with your goals. And we saw we just saw with the Blue Origin rocket exploding just how risky space is. We're just trying to get people to step on the moon again. And it's this difficult. Again, not to say he's not right, but is it is it the time frame? I heard something great when you're talking about the selling pressure. The the retail IPO is the exit liquidity for the private market, right? So they took the early punt and this is their opportunity to kind of cash out, if you will. So then now you're on the next wave of investing in this and do you get that same return? I mean, I think that's what we've got to try to think about. And I think it's I like the way you put it cuz it's not it's not critical of the businesses, it's just your investing framework. Is it fitting in your investing framework and keeping that in mind? Um when we're talking about value investing, I think this is a really interesting time because there seems to be when I hear people bring it up and I will get questions in chats and we'll get people emailing us questions and and and you can tell they come from a a value framework as well, but there's frustration, right? And sometimes it seems like there is what about this? Like I'm in these sectors and they're not doing anything. Consumer staples, we just I just got a question about that the other day. What about consumer staples? They're supposed to be a safe place to be. Um, and people think about it value investing that way and it seems like old economy areas maybe out of out of favor. What what is a value approach mean to you? How should we think about that? >> Yeah, I think a value approach doesn't not mean you cannot be in technology stocks at all. You look at Warren Buffett, he's now gone from Apple's to Alphabet, right? He's got his two A's in there. And and but I think if you listen to Buffett and also now Greg Abel who's running Berkshire, when they look at a company like Apple, it's really a consumer consumer almost a consumer discretionary parts of this almost consumer staple business. I mean every we have to have a a phone, right? You have an iPhone and and then you know, it's got this massive ecosystem in it. So it's got a moat around it. And so it's got some of these attributes of what you look for in a company. Not, you know, an oligopoly, there's not very many competitors. It's in a market niche that it controls. It's got you addicted because it's got all this integration between its products. I mean, I have an iPad and I have a MacBook. I have my iPhone. And you try to get out of that Yeah, exactly. Try to get out of that system. And it and it works really well. My wife just had to change her computer. And you know, you push a few buttons and it loads and transfers over the next one and it all communicates with you know, her other her other devices. It's fantastic, right? So, that actually is a powerful powerful business with large free cash flows and you can measure it and you can value it. But it's still in the technology space. I think Google, which they made a big investment in also Google/ Alphabet, you can again look at YouTube and you can look at their search business and you can look at their AI business and and you know, Waymo and so forth. And you start looking at those businesses, they're leaders and you know, one or two you know, one or two position in all of those areas. And so that is a business, a serious business with a massive moat and lots of cash flow coming in and and yet it's in sectors that are growing more quickly than the overall economy and that's clearly what you like to be ideally. So, I mean we always talk about it's it's it's better to be in a rising tide lifts all boats. >> Mhm. >> And so, if you can find secular growth in the economy, areas that are growing above the overall economic growth, then that is favorable for for leaders because they can then grow at 4 5 6 7 8% even though the economy might be growing at two. Uh and then they can they can grow their bottom line even faster. So, I think that you know, value value is about buying stocks you can understand. There's predictability, there's the the margins are generally predictable. You can go back and and you so you can do evaluation on them and um you know, they're you know, strong balance sheet and you can value them based upon cash flow or net asset value, you know, the values that's on the balance sheet. And that can be in any industry. And I think it but the Buffett shift as you've seen over the last little while, I think it indicates that he's quite comfortable in some of these companies that are tech companies, but these are leaders. They're really big, they're diversified uh revenue streams and uh they're fairly predictable. I mean, they have markets locked up for some time and if there is a problem with those businesses, generally you'll see an erosion and it gives you time to get out. You know, they're not going to blow up overnight, that's for sure. It'll take time for them to make bad decisions and then lose market share and then you can get out. So, that that's what we So, we have we've had exposure to Apple, we've had exposure to uh uh Google or Alphabet and uh even um uh Amazon. Uh our bigger positions right now in terms of tech and some of these bigger companies would really be Amazon and uh Apple in a secondary way. Um but uh and and again, you look at Amazon, it's trying to do some of that things in space. Blue Origin blew up, but they'll they'll they'll I think they'll get it right, but you're not really paying for it as much in Amazon. Um and so, I think there could be some hidden value there if they can get into that space. You'll want a couple players that are leaders. Um having said that, Elon Musk is a tough guy to keep up with. I mean, I I will admit that. He's a He's a driver. And uh he's he's uh he he's he's he's fantastic what he does, no question about it. >> Yeah. Well, and when you're public now, people are going to ask questions about how deep your bench is, right? Um you know, we know you want not a only a charismatic and visionary leader, you want it people who can also run the companies um below you. So, all that stuff starts to come into scrutiny now um as a public company. Um uh I By the way, Amazon, I love that you mentioned that because I've seen some uh and I'm I'm sure you watched this over the years. I mean, Amazon has had some you mentioned ups and downs, but you didn't have to buy that IPO. You had chances to get in because it what lost almost 70 it went down to some crazy price, and people kicked themselves for not taking that opportunity. So, you you sometimes that's a perfect example of getting another crack at it. >> Yeah, even in 2022 uh in '23 in that time frame approximately that time frame to go back, Amazon gave up about 40 45% of its value. Um it's phenomenal. That's just a few years ago. And what what was going on in Amazon? It continued to grow every quarter. Uh a bit of margin compression, but a lot of times when they're compressing their margins, it's because they're reinvesting back in their business to grab more market share. And so, when you're an investor, you're value investor, you're looking at, okay, not just the earnings, uh if they are plowing capital back into the business, what's the probability that capital that's going back into the business is going to generate a high return on equity. And so, that's new capital going back into the business. And if you're pretty certain that anytime they want, they can just increase their prices, which basically was the case with Amazon, and increase their margins when they wanted and they were doing that to crush the competitors. So, uh so, we felt comfortable paying a higher PE when we saw the price to revenue, and we looked at the kind of capital they were investing back in their business, and with the I think a uh very high degree of probability that that capital investment was going to result in great returns. So, again, you have to be you have to it can be can be careful. Think like a business person. They're They're building the you know, Jeff Bezos and his team they're they wanted something for the long haul, build something that was a behemoth. And you don't do that unless you invest back in the business and sometimes that will depress multiples. We have we have a small company and it's actually small, it's the leader in South America, Mercado Libre and that's similar to Amazon. They also have a financial service arm and they dominate through much of South America and we think that company's trading at a very low valuation. But again, what are they doing? They're investing a lot back in their business so they're depressing their earnings a little bit and the market goes, "Well, we don't like that." We go, "That's fine because they're building increased dominance. They're getting increased market share." And if they do that then they'll be able to solidify themselves in a leading leading position and compete with Amazon who's down there also, but Amazon hasn't had great success outside of North America to be honest with you. I mean, >> No, it's amazing a local competitor. You know, you you've got that local knowledge. That's so interesting. The one thing I think this is a great framework for everyone to to think about how you can how they can build their own framework, especially to be ready to talk to their advisor on the kinds of things to look for if they want to lean into value. The one thing I didn't hear you emphasize is price. Just because something is cheap, it doesn't sound like that is the value and that's what some people do, right? Oh, that's so beat up. I'm going to I'm going to invest in that. >> Yeah, Warren Buffett used to call that the the cigar butt approach to investing. You probably remember that term where he said, you know, you're walking along and you you see a cigar on the ground or something like that. You might get one puff out of it, but it might be free, it might be cheap, but you're not going to get much out of it, much satisfaction. So, sometimes it's better to if you're going to get a stogie, you get a full one and you can get you can get a longer longer time of puffing on it. So, yeah, you have to be careful. You can get into value traps also. So, a company could be trading at a low valuation. It might trade at a low valuation for a long time and go nowhere and that can be very frustrating. It's generally like to have a little momentum in the Or a takeout candidate sometimes merge M&A activity of course is we don't focus on this as much but that isn't that is a something that can expedite the price increase if it's an attractive asset it gets cheap and it could be taken out. Like we own one company Sprott. We own Sprott Inc which again controls a lot of ETFs. We think it's a great way to play the whole commodity space. But we also think over time that could be a very attractive takeout candidate and because they really got one of the best brands in that whole space. And so your best place to invest when you're investing in an asset manager is at the at the head you know at the company at the parent company which then has all of the fee based revenue coming up to it and the opportunities rather than in the individual funds. But that's a case there where we we own it for the long term prognosis but also that's very much a takeout candidate. >> Fantastic stuff. Jonathan this was I think such a great time to have this conversation and gave us a really good framework and a lot of things to think about as we see these sort of the IPO parade and all the hype around it. So appreciate >> that my here you are there's the ultimate value investing book. I've read parts of it I must say I haven't read it from cover to cover but Ben Graham and Graham Dodd and David Dodd security analysis that's the tome that if you're really serious about value investing you have to digest it a good chunk of that anyway. >> Fantastic and and who knows maybe you can get AI to give you a brief on it. So you know where to dive in. >> That's that's true you probably get AI to do some at least some distillations and there's there's nothing wrong with that but it would be good to work through the whole book if you're a student and you're doing your CFA. Yeah. >> Yeah got to go back to the basics. Jonathan thank you so much appreciate you always. >> Okay great thank you very much Maggie.
